ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish cooked with coconut milk
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for coconut rice; A0CKP (rice-based mixed dish) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for coconut rice as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of coconut rice is approximately 180g (about 1 cup cooked), as commonly plated in a nasi lemak portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut Rice (Nasi Lemak Rice Component) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Coconut Rice (cr_id G00010331), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this entry. Saturated fat estimated at 3.5g/100g based on coconut milk contribution as documented in HPB source notes; monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for coconut rice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is assigned based on the GI of white jasmine rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the primary carbohydrate component. Coconut milk does not materially lower the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.3g) / 100 ≈ 32.5, where carbohydrate per 180g serving = 25.19 × 1.8 = 45.3g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g (borderline high), partially offset by potassium 327mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Coconut rice contains 231mg sodium per 100g, which is borderline for hypertension management; however, the high potassium content (327mg/100g) and dietary fibre (3.7g/100g) provide meaningful cardiovascular offset — limit portion to 180g and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as sambal, salted fish, or fried anchovies to keep total meal sodium within recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~72) from white jasmine rice base; 25.2g carbohydrate per 100g yields high glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut rice has a high GI (~72) driven by the white jasmine rice base, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 32.5 per standard 180g serving, which will cause significant post-meal blood glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to no more than 90g, substitute brown rice or cauliflower rice where possible, and pair with high-fibre and high-protein accompaniments to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.5g/100g from coconut milk (lauric acid); cholesterol is 0mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coconut rice contributes approximately 3.5g saturated fat per 100g from coconut milk, primarily as lauric acid, which may raise LDL cholesterol with regular consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 180g or less, choose preparations using reduced-fat coconut milk, and ensure the overall meal pattern remains low in saturated fat and sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk does not qualify for MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out; high glycaemic load promotes systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coconut rice lacks omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other anti-inflammatory components; the saturated fat from coconut milk and the high glycaemic load from white rice may promote systemic inflammation, and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit portion size and prefer brown rice variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut rice (Malay: Nasi Lemak; Chinese: 椰浆饭) is a fragrant rice dish cooked by simmering white jasmine rice in coconut milk, typically with pandan leaves and salt, until the liquid is fully absorbed. It is the rice component of the iconic Singaporean and Malaysian dish nasi lemak, and is also served as a standalone side dish across Southeast Asia. The rice has a rich, creamy, slightly sweet flavour with a soft, slightly sticky texture imparted by the coconut milk fat. Per 100g, it provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat (predominantly saturated fat ~3.5g from coconut milk), and 25.19g carbohydrate with 3.7g dietary fibre. Sodium is 231mg/100g and potassium is notably high at 327mg/100g. The dish has a high glycaemic index (~72) due to the white jasmine rice base, making it a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The saturated fat content from coconut milk is a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegetarian, and vegan, and is halal-certified in most preparations. Coconut rice is a staple of Malay cuisine and is deeply embedded in Singaporean food culture, commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.
common_user_queries: How many calories in nasi lemak rice?
Is coconut rice good for diabetics?
What is the GI of coconut rice?
Is nasi lemak rice high in sodium?
Can I eat coconut rice if I have high blood pressure?
How much saturated fat is in coconut rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010331) with confidence 0.95 and provenance marked canonical. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution consistent with HPB source notes. GI assigned by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (white jasmine rice GI ~72), which is the standard defensible approach for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin profile, and moisture data for this coconut rice preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010331) as of 2026, due to limited published data for traditional preparations in this source. USDA FoodData Central was also consulted but does not contain a directly matching coconut rice entry with complete micronutrient breakdown. Saturated fat value of 3.5g/100g is estimated from the coconut milk contribution as documented in HPB source notes and is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for coconut milk-cooked rice. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g as confirmed by HPB source (plant-based dish with no animal fat). GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for coconut rice exists in the database. Glycaemic load per 180g serving calculated as (72 × 45.3) / 100 = 32.5.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 72 (high), gi_category 'high', glycemic_load_per_serving 32.5, gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate (white jasmine rice GI ~72). Estimation basis fully documented.
